title: Chapter 2 Quiz: How Large Language Models Work

questions.text: What is a 'token' in the context of LLMs?

options.text: A security password used to access the API
A small chunk of text (roughly 3–4 characters) that the model processes
A single complete word in the model's vocabulary
The final output message from the model

questions.explanation: Tokens are the fundamental units LLMs process. A token is roughly 3–4 characters or about ¾ of an average word. The word 'fascinating' might be split into 'fasci' and 'nating'. This is why API pricing is measured in tokens, not words.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'embeddings' in an LLM pipeline?

options.text: To store the model's responses in a permanent database
To convert tokens into numerical vectors that capture meaning and relationships
To split long documents into smaller sentences
To translate text between different human languages

questions.explanation: Embeddings map tokens into high-dimensional numerical vectors where meaning is encoded geometrically. Words with similar meanings have similar vectors — 'dog' and 'puppy' will be close together. This representation is what allows the model to reason about language.

questions.text: What does 'self-attention' allow a transformer to do?

options.text: Memorise entire documents verbatim for later retrieval
Generate images from text descriptions
Understand relationships between all words in a sentence simultaneously
Search the internet for relevant information in real time

questions.explanation: Self-attention calculates the relevance of every word to every other word in the input simultaneously — unlike older RNN models that processed words one at a time. This is what lets the model resolve pronouns, understand context, and handle long-range dependencies.

questions.text: What is RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)?

options.text: A technique where the model trains itself using automated rewards only
A process where human raters score responses to make the model more helpful and safe
A method for compressing model weights to reduce memory usage
A way to extend the model's context window beyond its training limit

questions.explanation: RLHF is the key step that turns a next-token predictor into a helpful assistant. Human reviewers rate model responses, and those ratings are used to train a 'reward model' that guides the LLM toward outputs that humans prefer — making it more helpful, honest, and less harmful.

questions.text: Why do two responses to the same prompt sometimes differ significantly?

options.text: The model connects to a different server with different weights each time
The user's internet connection speed affects the output quality
Token selection is probabilistic — the model samples from a distribution, not always picking the top token
The model randomly shuffles its training data at the start of each session

questions.explanation: At each step, the model produces a probability distribution over all possible next tokens. A parameter called 'temperature' controls how randomly the model samples from this distribution. Higher temperature = more variation and creativity. This is intentional — pure greedy selection (always picking the top token) produces repetitive, boring outputs.
